title: Cataloguing a magnetic tape with mixed-provenance tracks
intro: A question that comes up on the Records in Contexts users forum: an ethnographic magnetic tape holds several tracks, and the tracks have mixed provenance — different creators, places, dates and topics. Should the tape be a Record and each track a Record Part? This wizard walks the decision step by step, explains why each option fits or doesn't, and — with an API key — builds the model live against the OpenRiC reference server.
question: From a RiC perspective, is it appropriate to use the Record entity for the magnetic tape as a whole and the Record Part entity for each track, since the tracks often have mixed provenance (different agents, places, dates, topics)?

steps.prompt: First, the tape as a whole — its recorded content. How do you represent it?

choices.label: Record
choices.why: Fits. A Record is “discrete information content … inscribed on a carrier” (RiC-E04). The tape's recording is exactly that: one intellectual unit of content. It becomes the parent that the tracks attach to.

choices.label: Record Set
choices.why: Doesn't fit (yet). A Record Set (RiC-E03) is an aggregation of records grouped by shared attributes. A single physical tape isn't an aggregation of independent records — modelling it as a Set over-structures it. (A Set becomes right only if the tracks are genuinely standalone works; see the track step.)

choices.label: Record Part
choices.why: Doesn't fit. A Record Part (RiC-E05) is a component of a Record — it cannot stand as the whole. The tracks will be the parts; the tape is their whole.

choices.label: Instantiation
choices.why: Doesn't fit here. The Instantiation (RiC-E06) is the physical reel — the carrier, not the intellectual content. You'll add that in a later step, linked to this Record.

capture.comment: Create the tape as a Record (item-level information_object).

steps.prompt: Each track was recorded by different people, in different places, on different dates, on different topics. How do you represent a single track?
steps.note: Record Part vs separate Records — the test is intellectual independence, not “different agents.” A Record Part (RiC-E05) may carry its own creator, place and date, so mixed provenance alone doesn't force separate Records. Use Record Parts when the tracks are components of one tape-as-record (this scenario). Prefer separate Records (RiC-E04) grouped in a Record Set (RiC-E03) — with the reel as a shared Instantiation (RiC-E06) — when each track is a standalone work that would be searched, cited or appraised on its own.

choices.label: Record Part
choices.why: Fits. A Record Part is “a component of a Record with independent information content that contributes to the intellectual completeness of the Record” (RiC-E05). Each track contributes to the whole tape yet carries its own provenance — precisely the mixed-provenance case. It links to the tape as a part/whole relation.

choices.label: Record
choices.why: Usually doesn't fit. Modelling each track as a standalone Record loses that the tracks share one physical carrier and one intellectual whole. Choose this only if a track is genuinely an independent work you want catalogued on its own — then group the tracks under a Record Set instead of a single Record.

choices.label: Record Set
choices.why: Doesn't fit. A single track is not an aggregation of records, so it isn't a Record Set.

choices.label: Instantiation
choices.why: Doesn't fit. Instantiation is the physical carrier, not the track's intellectual content.

capture.comment: Create the track as a Record Part belonging to the tape. The parent_id is the RiC part/whole link — it binds the track to its tape.

steps.prompt: Track A was recorded by a different ethnographer than the other tracks. How do you attach that creator to this track only?

choices.label: Create an Agent, link it to the track with has_creator
choices.why: Fits. Per-part provenance is expressed as relations on the Record Part itself, not as attributes. Create the ethnographer as an Agent, then relate the track to that Agent with has_creator (RiC-R027). Because the relation is on the part, each track can have its own, different creator — the whole point of using Record Parts here.

choices.label: Put the creator's name in the tape's title
choices.why: Doesn't fit. Names buried in free-text titles aren't reconciliable, can't be shared as authorities, and can't differ per track. RiC wants the creator as a first-class Agent linked by a relation.

choices.label: Set one creator on the tape for all tracks
choices.why: Doesn't fit the scenario. A single creator on the tape erases the mixed provenance you set out to capture. Attach the creator to the individual Record Part instead.

capture.comment: Create the ethnographer as an Agent.
Link the track (subject) to the Agent (object) with the has_creator relation. Repeat per track with its own Agent, place, date and subject.

steps.prompt: Finally, the physical reel itself — ¼-inch open-reel tape, 7.5 ips, some vinegar syndrome. Where does that go?

choices.label: Instantiation
choices.why: Fits. The Instantiation (RiC-E06) is the physical/digital carrier of the Record — format, extent, playback characteristics and condition live here, linked to the tape Record. This is why “the tape as a whole” split into two: the intellectual Record and its physical Instantiation.

choices.label: Record Part
choices.why: Doesn't fit. A Record Part is intellectual content (a track), not the physical object carrying it.

choices.label: An attribute on the Record
choices.why: Doesn't fit well. Carrier format and condition belong on the Instantiation so that multiple carriers (an original reel and a digitised copy) can each be described and compared.

capture.comment: Create the physical carrier as an Instantiation. (Link it to the tape Record the same way — a relation — once your server exposes a carrier relation type.)

outcome.summary: The tape is a Record; each track is a Record Part bound to it by the part/whole link; each track's own creator, place, date and subject are relations on that part; and the physical reel is an Instantiation. That structure captures mixed per-track provenance without flattening the tape into unrelated items.
